---
title: "The Augmented Synthetic Historical Control Method"
format: 
  pdf:
    documentclass: article
    geometry: margin=1in
    include-in-header: prelim.tex
    keep-tex: true
    cite-method: natbib
    pdf-engine: pdflatex   # force pdfLaTeX instead of XeLaTeX
bibliography: pl.bib
---

# Introduction

The synthetic control method (SCM) of \citet{Abadie2010} is one of the most widely used approaches in panel data econometrics for estimating treatment effects, second only to difference-in-differences. The idea behind SCM is simple: the untreated potential outcomes of a treated unit can be approximated by a weighted average of donor pool units (i.e., untreated or unexposed entities). Numerous extensions have been proposed, including singular value decomposition \citep{Amjad2018,GSCMIT}, penalized regression models \citep{scmdisagg}, Bayesian methods \citep{bayesscpen,KimLee2020}, and further refinements \citep{Abadie2021}.

Like difference-in-differences, SCM requires a suitable donor pool. In many cases, however, no appropriate donors exist—such as during global events (e.g., pandemics, financial crises) or when comparable untreated units cannot be observed. To address this, \citet{SHC} introduced the synthetic historical control method (SHC). Rather than relying on external donors, SHC uses the treated unit’s own pre-treatment history: the pre-treatment period is split into overlapping segments, which serve as a donor pool. A quadratic program then estimates weights over these segments to predict counterfactual outcomes, leveraging the idea that future outcomes resemble past patterns. SHC, like \citet{Abadie2010,Abadie2015}, restricts weights to the simplex, ensuring convex combinations of donor segments. This guarantees interpretability but can be too restrictive in time-series settings where noise or complex dynamics make exact pre-treatment fit unattainable. In such cases, purely convex weights may not provide accurate approximations.

This paper introduces the *Augmented Synthetic Historical Control* method (ASHC). ASHC extends SHC by \cite{SHC} in two key ways. First, it relaxes the simplex constraint to an affine constraint, requiring only that weights sum to one. This allows for negative weights, permitting extrapolation beyond the convex hull of observed segments in exchange for better fit. Second, it adds a quadratic proximity penalty that discourages large deviations from the SHC solution, in the spirit of the augmented SCM of \citet{BenMichael2021}, which encourages the estimator to standard SCM unless extrapolation is needed. ASHC retains, however, the stabilizing Gram penalty from \citet{SHC}, which regularizes low-variance directions in the donor matrix. Together, these three components yield a new estimator that generalizes SHC to settings of imperfect pre-treatment fit.

We establish the theoretical properties of ASHC. We prove a finite-sample error bound, decomposing the error into four interpretable parts: SHC residual error, a regularization-controlled deviation term, smoothness-induced bias, and noise. We then show that ASHC is asymptotically consistent: as pre-treatment length and smoothing order grow, prediction error converges to the noise floor, and under sub-Gaussian noise, to zero in probability. Finally, we derive the convergence rate, finding that under the optimal bandwidth, the error decays approaches a near-parametric rate. Together, these results demonstrate that ASHC preserves the interpretability of SHC while offering provably stronger error control and asymptotic guarantees.

Section 2 introduces the assumptions and implementation of the SHC method. Then, I introduce the ASHC method. Section 3 presents the finite sample and asymptotic analysis. Section 4 presents calibrated simulations. Section 5 presents an empirical application. All proofs are presented in the appendix. The implementation of this was carried out using Python's mlsynth library (https://mlsynth.readthedocs.io/en/latest/).
